sentences.text: Yeah, I remember--
It happpend on the 18th of January '45.
It started in the morning.
We saw all this kind of marching and people were cold, and we had to take one blanket.
And we went out before the gate, and they were distributing for each one, one or two loafs of bread and that was all.
And they were counting us endlessly, until it was dark.
And I remember when it started to get dark, we were marching out.
And I remember even time-- short time before this started, to demolished the crematorium.
I remember the crematorium running too.
Then crematorium was anyhow demolished partly from before.
But more and more demolition.
And just when we are leaving, we could hear how they put-- must have been dynamite or what--
And they demolished the crematorium [INAUDIBLE].
And I remember also that there was tremendous chaos at the evacuation, and that enabled these few-- I imagine there were about 20 or so-- Sonderkommando people to somehow enter our line.
And the SS were looking for them.
Because in this chaos, they just mixed with us--
And in this way, they escaped from Auschwitz with us.
The idea of the Germans was to kill the Sonderkommados.
Of course.
They were the very first in the list to be annihilated.
In order that they won't tell what happened.
There was anyhow not many left after the uprising.
They left only a small group for their immediate need for burning dead corpse.
I don't think there were much gassing afterwards.
Maybe they were shooting, killing some of them.
About 20.
And I remember I met a few of them.
Yeah.
Two or three I met in Mount Hausen in the beginning.
Then I left.
Well, I suffered a lot in [INAUDIBLE] in Auschwitz.
But it was peanuts from the time from the todesmaersche.
What we called the todesmaersche, the marching.
Why?
It was a very, very severe winter.
And in the beginning, I think it was three days we had to march, more or less without any rest, day and night.
And we march, and we marched.
And there was colonies of other people, and all that we could see next to us on both sides people who were shot.
Lying near the [INAUDIBLE] on the [INAUDIBLE] right and left.
And this gray mass of the brain running out from the-- from-- from the skull.
And we knew, if we stop, that is our death.
And also, if you are in the last row-- because people slowly, slowly couldn't go any further, and were behind.
And the ones that were behind were shot.
Or the ones who sat down.
Also it is winter and terribly tired.
And we were half dreaming because we couldn't sleep and we had to go on.
Our luck was that we, I mean the children, helped as far as we could--
Each other we gave a hand.
Not many-- there were not many left.
Because we lost track.
But lets say a group of 10 children were more or less in the same colony.
And we helped each other as far as we could.
But the immediate danger and slowly losing our-- our strengths, and always hearing this shouting around and killing, seeing the dead corpse, and SS around us, [GERMAN].
And so on.
Yes, of course they were SS people were walking with us.
And we know that is-- that is our end if we stop or if we are left behind.
We didn't know.
We went and it was day and night.
After three days, we came to Blechhammer, which was a camp.
Blechhammer.
Blechhammer was one of the out-- in Silesia.
Where was it?
In Germany.
In Silesia.
In Poland, yeah.
And all that I remember, as I told you, it was so horrible, that I could say this--
On the way to Blechhmaer.
Thank God that my father was gone.
He wouldn't be able in his age and in his condition-- he was nearly a Muselmann-- to survive this march, and he would surely be killed like everyone who couldn't go any further.
And I thought it's more, a kind of better death, I thought at the time.
Not to suffer because I hardly could endure it.
And I remember also that next to us [INAUDIBLE] were the children from Lodz, or Litzmannstadt, and later on they told us this story how it was in the Lodz.
And they had it-- well, they had it from years, since '39, a very, very bad time.
And even in Lodz, they suffered enormously, much more than us physically.
I mean, we were left from many, many thousands of children.
But at least, comparatively, we had it better.
They were starved, and one of the children told me, well, if somebody was very sick, the doctor in Lodz prescribed him 200 grams or something like that of rotten potato peels.
That's what they could give him as an extra kind of ration.
Even before, because they were with us, and they came with us out.
So they didn't have so much strength as us.
Somehow we arrived in Blechhammer.
We had a night, maybe, of rest, and then came the [INAUDIBLE] day, which was Sonderdienst, a special unit of the SS, of German.
Sonderdienst, they usually were designed for the worst kind of jobs, and in a very cruel way, they again--
Took us for a march, and we came to a railway station, and we were put into wagons.
Cattle wagons.
But some were closed and some were open.
And our luck that we children had it again a little better because we came in this very severe winter in a closed cattle wagon, which again meant better chance for surviving.
The condition there were really horrible.
In the waggon.
But again, we were lucky that we were between us, only--
I can't say, but there were about 80 or 100 in one wagon.
There was two or three wagons like that, but there were some open wagons.
In this cold
I knew afterwards that the ones that were in open wagons were--
Frozen.
Many of them were frozen to death.
In some wagon, where Russian former POV, or older prisoners--
Prisoners, yes.
--They squeezed-- non Jews-- the Jews, let's say they squeezed all of the Jews into half of the waggon and they took the rest.
Which again meant for the people who were left, this horrible condition, death.
And so died my friend [NON-ENGLISH], I told you about.
He was in such a cattle wagon with other prisoners, older men, who took advantage that they were stronger and they took from the children--
Their place.
Their place, which meant death.
We didn't get at the beginning any food, any water.
And I remember--
In the train.
We found some empty conserves, and some kind of drinks.
And somehow we had it with us, and we somehow threw it out from the wagon and could collect a bit of snow, which we melted, and that was our water.
Later on, on the way, it was again of luck, I remember, near Katowice, suddenly there was some possibly English airplanes, bombed a train of German soldiers who were next to us.
And some of the soldiers went under our trains because they hoped this way they would be safe.
But by machine guns they killed them, and of course many of us.
Again, what meant luck-- in these wagons there was a tiny little window and everyone tried to be near this window to have a bit of fresh air, and we took turns.
I remember just I had my turn, but I gave it to somebody else, to a friend of mine, and exactly where he stood, he was shot to death.
A bullet.
And somebody else from our group was also wounded or killed.
Anyhow, in the next station they took the dead people out and just throw them out.
I don't know what happened.
We passed again, because it was a very well known railway connection in the station, [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH].
Yeah, which now was the second time, or third time I passed it.
When I went to Theresienstadt, from Theresienstadt to Auschwitz, and now from Auschwitz to-- on the way to I didn't know where.
But anyhow, we stopped there.
It was the early morning.
People, workers, went to work.
And they saw us.
They were on a little bridge like on the railway station, and they had their lunch and they throw it to us.
And especially I could somehow to communicate with them, because they couldn't guard the whole train.
I spoke the language and therefore, into our wagon they brought us a bit of coffee.
Their own--

Well, they couldn't have their eyes everywhere, and because it was mixed with civil population, somehow we got some food into our wagon, which surely saved us, because the journey continued quite a long time.
I remember we passed Prague.
We were somewhere in a railway station near Prague.
We went further on.
On the way, one SS let us once out to make toilet.

Before, what we could do is when-- I mean, I told you we had this melted snow, and we took our blankets and we made little square pieces like a handkerchief and as long as there was something left of the blankets, we went on the toilet on these blankets and we throw it out.
But later, even this we couldn't do.
No, nothing.
But that was that.
After quite a long time, it was about I guess a week or maybe longer, we arrived at Mauthausen.
I didn't know that it's Mauthausen.
But we arrived in Austria, and I still mostly had some aesthetical something in me and I looked.
It was completely different from Auschwitz, because there were mountains and the buildings were from stones.
You know in Mauthausen, they have quarries.
And everything was made from stone.
It was completely different.
In the looks from Birkenau--
And the surrounding was beautiful.
And it was a sunny day and blue skies.
But when we arrived--
It was the end of January.
I guess around the 25 of January, something like that.
But we had to walk quite a while.
Many couldn't do it.
And we saw again the prisoners and we came to Mauthausen.
And there, there was some very, for us, old prisoner, meaning there many years.
Some were even 12 years since '39, political prisoners and so on.
And I remember one was even a Czech kind of kapo.
He was a good man.
But his nerves were in such a stage.
I've never seen somebody so nervous.
A bundle of nerves.
But he didn't beat us.
So he behaved.
They put us in the beginning in barracks, and slowly they divided us into little groups.
And they older ones had to do all kinds of works.
But I remember the conditions were horrible.
Because we slept somehow on the floor.
Slept is understatement.
Like sardines.
I mean my head on foot of somebody else.
So always head and foot, head and foot.
And they were squeezing us really like sardines.
The trouble was if somebody wanted to get out at night, he couldn't more squeeze himself into the last position.
Then there was a shortage of water and of food.
We had less and less food.
But I do remember, we came to again a closed block.
The blockeldester was [INAUDIBLE] against [GERMAN].
[GERMAN] was a kind of the second after the blockeldester.
Both were German criminals.
Sadists like that.
I didn't--
Sadists.
--so before.
They behaved in the most outrageous cruel way.
They [INAUDIBLE] us and--
Always they wanted to clean the block, so it doesn't matter if it was snow or terrible weather, we had to go out and some of them had to clean the floor in this block.
We meanwhile could die of cold and this terrible winter.
The children and grown ups, partisans from Yugoslavia, I remember.
And he was behaving in most cruel way.

And I remember his speech, when he said-- because at that time, they knew the war is going slowly to the end-- when the war-- when the German-- oh, sorry-- when the Russian will be five kilometers from here, I will take my knife, he would say, [GERMAN], he was--
A hairdresser.
And all of you, I will kill you until your Jewish blood will be sprinkling here on the wall.
And terrible speeches.
That was the second.
But also the block was--
German criminals.
I know only [NON-ENGLISH].
The second name, no.
I knew it-- and I own knew that he was lynched after the liberation.
So we have been in this block.
But even in this block-- of course, what happened when we came, we went again through [INAUDIBLE].
They took everything we had.
I remember I was very, very clever, and even from Auschwitz, I managed to smuggle a box with cigarettes, some 20 cigarettes.
And that was in my shoes.
How old were you at that time?
At that time, I was barely 15.
Something like that.
No, no.
But I smuggled the cigarettes and I somehow was able to bring some to Mauthausen.
And AT Mauthausen I managed to change it for a special, I don't know--
Two or three rations of soup.
And in Mauthausen, there were many ration, and POVs.
They were treated in the most cruel way.
Sometimes the German led them naked and pour on them cold-- I mean water they were frozen to death and so on.
I remember there was a little--
Surely, 10, 15 under.
And I remember, there were Czech, non Jews, prisoners, political prisoners, who behaved most wonderfully to us, and they let us have their ration of soup.
And it was whole procedure how they've done it.
They were living in different compounds of course, and they left the soup under the bed.
You must imagine, only in a block which was more or less in better condition, somebody left his ration of soup--
--it would be impossible to do it in Auschwitz or in late in Birkenau or in the Mauthausen between us.
But the old prisoners had their own blocks where their situation was much better.
So he told us I go to work but I leave you children some soup.
And it was a whole job to get out of our block of our courtyard, but we managed.
And we took the soup.
And we brought it to our camp.
Once it was me.
And I remember the greatest temptation in my life was that I had now this soup and I had to share it--
A pot-- I don't know, a little soup.
But at that time, it was like gold.
And I knew everyone had four spoons let's say, of soup.
We could divide it between our little group of children.
I had my four spoons.
I was able to finish the whole soup.
I was able to eat five spoons and it was the greatest temptation in my life not to do it and to share it.
Because it's very difficult to imagine what it is if you're in such a situation and have food to share it with somebody else.
But we still had this kind of--
Much worse.
I mean, people were dying of hunger.
And even the beginning wasn't so bad to say months or two months later.
I speak now about the end of--
--January, beginning of February.
We had a sadist blockeldester.
He took our rations.
Put it in a blanket, trampled on it.
So they were just crumbs.
And sometimes the crumbs were [NON-ENGLISH], how you call it, [NON-ENGLISH] with--
Anyway, they were not edible.
You couldn't eat it.
I mean, we couldn't eat it, but then what he gave us and even these few crumbs, they were tasteless, and we had to sell a few of these crumbs to get a few drops of salt, et cetera.
But he beat up people in the most-- the shouting of the poor Jews who were beaten by him, it's unforgettable.
He was really a sadist.
That was Mauthausen.
But that was only the beginning.
Later on, it was worse and worse, because there was evacuation of all camps from the east, and they were brought to the west.
All the former concentration camps were over-- overfilled with prisoners.
Yeah, I'm speaking of Mauthausen.
There came more and more transports.
And then there came transports from Hungarian Jews and women.
And this was the so-called Munka Tabor.
Munka Tabor is [INAUDIBLE] Hungarian working camps.
And they came also by Todesmarsche and so on or by train to Mauthausen.
And Mauthausen was overfilled.
Some people went further on to [INAUDIBLE] and other camps.
And they had to work in these under--
Yeah, in mines.
And this factory where they have-- where they produce parts of the firearms, the rockets, in horrible condition under the ground.
We, who were a little group of children, were lucky we were staying in Mauthausen.
At least not all of us went to a very severe work.
Sometimes we didn't went at all to work.
But slowly, we were losing weight and energy and even then we were transported to another camp in my Mauthausen, the so-called Zelt.
Zelt means--
A huge tent.
And these conditions there were indescribable.
Really without food, without anything.
Head full of lices.
I remember I couldn't get up when I was lying in the mud or whatever.
I did try to just to sit down, I was just white for my eyes.
We were slowly dying, and around people were dying.
Then I remember one date it was bombardment by the Americans and our [GERMAN] was near a SS camp.
And the bomb fell seconds before and fell into our camp.
And afterwards, there were cases which I saw of cannibalism.
Yeah, I mean, there were dead bodies all around, and the prisoners who were a little stronger took hold of them and one who--
Of the pieces of the bodies.
And I saw it as some of them tried even to boil it.
I wouldn't say because I don't know.
Well, they ate it.
Pieces of meat, as food and they ate it.
Yes.
The ones who had the possibility, they cooked it.
One of a very young prisoner who told me proudly-- was very primitive and there were many in the camps-- and he tod me, oh, look how lucky I was.
There was just some bodies in the [GERMAN], which nobody got it, and I was one of the first.
And I could take off a bit from the hips, which means there was still not only bones, but a little flesh left.
That was his great luck.
So people were starving.
Of course, yeah.
Yeah.
Not only me there were many who saw it.
No.
I saw it with my own eyes.
Well, you see something which is looking like some pieces of meat.
And there were many other prisoners who tried to find some food.
And we children tried to find some-- some-- something which was still growing, grass.
But even there was no grass.
It was a severe winter.
Sometimes we found the [NON-ENGLISH], the-- how you call it?
Off the grass, which is inside.
The part that is inside the earth.
The part that is inside the earth.
Sometimes we were lucky.
We found something we could eat.
The hunger was undescribable.

I remember people who came from Hungary and had some golden rings, which the Germany and his great cowardice, didn't succeed yet to take away, took their golden rings and gave it just for two spoons of soup.
I remember--
Yeah.
